stem_template: A company's balance sheet shows: Non-current assets ${{nca}}, Current assets ${{ca}}, Non-current liabilities ${{ncl}}, Current liabilities ${{cl}}, Share capital ${{sc}}, Retained earnings ${{re}}. Verify the balance sheet equation and calculate: (a) net assets, (b) working capital.

re.formula: nca + ca - ncl - cl - Math.floor((nca + ca - ncl - cl) * 0.6)

answer_template: Net Assets = (NCA + CA) − (NCL + CL) = ${{nca}} + ${{ca}} − ${{ncl}} − ${{cl}} = ${{net_assets}}. Working Capital = CA − CL = ${{ca}} − ${{cl}} = ${{wc}}. Balance sheet: Assets = Liabilities + Equity → ${{nca}} + ${{ca}} = ${{ncl}} + ${{cl}} + ${{sc}} + ${{re}} ✓
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark net assets formula; 1 mark correct net assets value; 1 mark working capital formula; 1 mark balance sheet equation verified. Max 4.
hints: Balance sheet equation: Total Assets = Total Liabilities + Equity.
Working capital = Current Assets − Current Liabilities. Positive = liquid.

subject: business
title: Efficiency Ratio Analysis
stem_template: {{company}} has: Sales ${{sales}}, COGS ${{cogs}}, Debtors ${{debtors}}, Stock ${{stock}}, Creditors ${{creditors}}. Calculate all three efficiency ratios and evaluate the company's working capital management.
answer_template: Debtor days = ({{debtors}}/{{sales}}) × 365 = {{dd}} days. Stock turnover = {{cogs}}/{{stock}} = {{st}} times. Creditor days = ({{creditors}}/{{cogs}}) × 365 = {{cd}} days. Good WCM: debtor days < creditor days (collect before you pay); high stock turnover (fast-moving inventory). If {{dd}} > {{cd}}, funding gap exists — potential cash flow pressure.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark each ratio calculation (max 3); 1 mark comparison/evaluation. Max 4.
hints: Creditor days = (Creditors/COGS) × 365.
Funding gap = Debtor days + Stock days − Creditor days.

subject: business
title: Cash Flow Problem Solutions
stem_template: {{company}} has a negative closing cash balance of ${{deficit}} in Month {{month}}. Identify three causes of this cash flow problem and recommend two solutions.
answer_template: Causes: (1) Sales on credit — debtors delay payments while {{company}} has immediate expenses. (2) High fixed costs (rent, salaries) relative to early-stage revenue. (3) Seasonal demand — revenue low in Month {{month}} but costs continue. Solutions: (1) Negotiate extended payment terms with suppliers (increase creditor days). (2) Offer early payment discounts to debtors to accelerate receipts. (3) Arrange an overdraft facility or short-term loan for the deficit period. (4) Reduce discretionary spending temporarily.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark per cause (max 3); 1 mark per solution with justification (max 2). Max 5.
hints: Cash flow problems ≠ losses. A profitable business can be cash-flow negative.
Solutions: speed up receipts, slow down payments, or arrange short-term finance.

subject: business
title: Market Share Calculation
stem_template: The total market for {{product}} is ${{total_market}}m annually. {{company}} has sales of ${{company_sales}}m. What is {{company}}'s market share?
answer_template: Market share = (Company sales / Total market) × 100 = (${{company_sales}} / ${{total_market}}) × 100 = {{ms}}%.
distractor_templates: Market share = Total market / Company sales × 100.
Market share = Company sales × Total market.
Market share = Company sales / Total market (without × 100).

options_template.text: Market share = (Company sales / Total market) × 100 = {{ms}}%.
Total market / Company sales × 100.
Company sales × Total market.
(Company sales / Total market) without × 100.

mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark correct formula; 1 mark correct answer {{ms}}%.
hints: Market share = (Your sales / Total market sales) × 100.
High market share often indicates competitive advantage.

subject: business
title: Primary vs Secondary Market Research
stem_template: A company conducts focus groups with {{n}} target customers to understand preferences for a new product. This is an example of:
answer_template: Primary (field) research — original data collected directly from respondents for a specific research purpose; includes surveys, interviews, focus groups, and observations.
distractor_templates: Secondary (desk) research — using existing published data sources.
Qualitative secondary research — analysing competitors' annual reports.
Experimental research — A/B testing using historical sales data.

options_template.text: Primary research — original data collected directly from target customers (focus groups).
Secondary research — existing published data.
Qualitative secondary research — competitor reports.
Experimental research — historical A/B testing.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for primary research + original data + direct from respondents.
hints: Primary = collected first-hand for this specific purpose.
Secondary = data that already exists (reports, censuses, competitor data).

subject: business
title: Seasonal Variation in Sales Forecasting
stem_template: A company notices that sales of {{product}} are consistently {{direction}} in Q{{quarter}} each year. This regular pattern is called:
answer_template: Seasonal variation — a regular, predictable fluctuation in sales tied to the time of year; identified using moving averages to separate the trend from seasonal effects.
distractor_templates: Cyclical variation — long-term economic cycles affecting demand.
Random variation — unpredictable, one-off fluctuations with no pattern.
Secular trend — long-term underlying upward or downward movement.

options_template.text: Seasonal variation — regular predictable fluctuation; identified via moving averages.
Cyclical variation — long-term economic cycle effects.
Random variation — unpredictable one-off fluctuations.
Secular trend — long-term underlying movement.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for seasonal variation + regular/predictable + time-of-year pattern.
hints: Seasonal variation is regular and predictable — it repeats each year.
Moving averages remove seasonal effects to reveal the underlying trend.

subject: business
title: Distribution Channels
stem_template: A manufacturer of {{product}} sells directly to consumers through its own website, bypassing wholesalers and retailers. This is called:
answer_template: Direct distribution (zero-level channel) — manufacturer sells directly to consumer without intermediaries; increases control over brand experience and margins, but requires investment in own distribution infrastructure.
distractor_templates: Indirect distribution — manufacturer uses wholesalers and retailers.
Multi-channel distribution — selling through multiple channels simultaneously.
Franchised distribution — selling through licensed franchise outlets.

options_template.text: Direct distribution (zero-level channel) — no intermediaries; higher control and margin.
Indirect distribution — uses wholesalers/retailers.
Multi-channel distribution — multiple simultaneous channels.
Franchised distribution — through licensed outlets.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for direct distribution/zero-level channel + no intermediaries.
hints: Zero-level: Manufacturer → Consumer. One-level: Manufacturer → Retailer → Consumer.
Direct distribution: more control, higher margin, but higher logistics cost.

subject: business
title: Process in Service Marketing
stem_template: A hospital implements a new patient booking system that reduces waiting times from {{old_wait}} to {{new_wait}} minutes. This improvement relates to which element of the 7Ps?
answer_template: Process — the systems and procedures used to deliver a service; improving the booking process directly enhances customer experience and service efficiency.
distractor_templates: People — staff training and attitude.
Physical evidence — tangible elements like facilities and branding.
Product — the core medical service offered.

options_template.text: Process — systems and procedures for service delivery; booking system = process improvement.
People — staff training and attitude.
Physical evidence — tangible elements.
Product — core medical service.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for process + service delivery systems/procedures.
hints: Process: how the service is delivered (booking, queuing, service flow).
Physical evidence: what customers can see/touch (building, brochure, uniform).

subject: business
title: Flow Production Characteristics
stem_template: A {{product}} production line runs 24 hours a day, producing identical items continuously on an automated assembly line. This is:
answer_template: Flow (mass/continuous) production — large volumes of identical products manufactured continuously using highly automated machinery; lowest cost per unit due to economies of scale but high capital investment and inflexible.
distractor_templates: Job production — each unit made individually to unique specifications.
Batch production — set quantities made before switching to another product.
Cell production — small teams complete all stages for groups of products.

options_template.text: Flow (mass) production — continuous, automated, identical products, lowest unit cost.
Job production — individual unique items.
Batch production — set quantities then switch.
Cell production — team-based complete manufacture.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for flow/mass/continuous production + correct characteristics (continuous, automated, high volume, identical).
hints: Flow production: 24/7 operation, highest volume, lowest cost/unit, least flexible.
Examples: car assembly (semi-continuous), oil refining, bottled water.

subject: business
title: Lean Production Evaluation
stem_template: {{manufacturer}}, a {{product}} manufacturer, is considering implementing a full lean production system including JIT, kaizen, and TQM. Evaluate the potential benefits and challenges.
answer_template: Benefits: JIT eliminates stock costs and working capital tied up in inventory; kaizen continuously reduces waste (muda) through employee-led improvements; TQM reduces defect rates and rework costs; combined, these reduce costs, improve quality, and increase customer satisfaction. Challenges: JIT requires very reliable suppliers (any disruption halts production — vulnerability shown in COVID-19 supply chain crisis); kaizen requires cultural change (employee buy-in, management commitment); TQM is time-consuming to implement; initial training and system setup costs are high. Overall: lean is beneficial for high-volume, stable-demand manufacturing but risky in volatile supply environments.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark JIT benefit; 1 mark kaizen benefit; 1 mark TQM benefit; 1 mark supply chain risk; 1 mark cultural challenge; 1 mark conclusion. Max 6.
hints: Lean production eliminates all forms of waste (muda: overproduction, waiting, transport, inventory, motion, defects, overprocessing).
COVID-19 exposed JIT vulnerability — global supply chain disruptions.

subject: business
title: ISO Certification
stem_template: A company obtains ISO {{standard}} certification. What does this signify to its customers and stakeholders?
answer_template: ISO {{standard}} certification signifies that the company has independently verified systems and processes meeting internationally recognised standards; it provides assurance of quality/environmental/security management practices to customers, suppliers, and regulators.
distractor_templates: That the company's products are guaranteed to be defect-free.
That the company has the lowest prices in its industry.
That the company is owned by the International Organisation for Standardisation.

options_template.text: ISO {{standard}} confirms independently verified management systems meeting international standards.
Products guaranteed defect-free.
Company has lowest industry prices.
Company is owned by ISO.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for independently verified management systems + international standards + stakeholder assurance.
hints: ISO certification: third-party audit confirms processes meet standards.
ISO 9001 is the most common quality management standard globally.

subject: business
title: Offshoring vs Outsourcing
stem_template: {{company}} moves its customer service operations to {{country}} where labour costs are lower, but keeps these operations in-house (not given to an external firm). This is:
answer_template: Offshoring (not outsourcing) — the company relocates its own operations to a lower-cost country but retains internal control; outsourcing would mean contracting an external third party to perform the function.
distractor_templates: Outsourcing — giving the function to an external specialist firm.
Domestic relocation — moving operations within the same country.
Franchising — licensing the operation to a local business in {{country}}.

options_template.text: Offshoring — relocating own operations to lower-cost country; still internal control.
Outsourcing — contracting an external firm.
Domestic relocation — moving within same country.
Franchising — licensing to a local business.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for offshoring + internal control maintained + lower-cost country.
hints: Offshoring = move location; still internal. Outsourcing = give to external firm.
A firm can offshore AND outsource simultaneously (offshore outsourcing).

subject: business
title: Outsourcing Advantages
stem_template: A company outsources its {{function}} to a specialist provider. What is the primary benefit of this decision?
answer_template: Access to specialist expertise and economies of scale without the overhead of maintaining an in-house team — the outsourcing provider's specialisation and scale means lower cost and higher quality than the company could achieve internally.
distractor_templates: The company retains full control over the quality and speed of {{function}}.
Outsourcing always results in job creation within the company.
The company eliminates all risks associated with {{function}}.

options_template.text: Access to specialist expertise and scale economies without in-house overhead.
Full control over quality and speed maintained.
Creates more jobs within the company.
Eliminates all risks associated with {{function}}.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for specialist expertise + cost/quality benefit vs in-house.
hints: Outsourcing: buy the expertise externally rather than build it internally.
Disadvantage: loss of control over quality and confidentiality risks.

subject: business
title: Marketing Mix (4Ps)
stem_template: Which of the following are elements of the Marketing Mix (4Ps)? Select ALL that apply.

options_template.text: Product
People
Price
Place (distribution)
Promotion
Process

mark_scheme_template: 4Ps: Product, Price, Place, Promotion. People and Process are part of the extended 7Ps (services marketing) but not the core 4Ps.
hints: 4Ps = Product, Price, Place, Promotion.
7Ps adds People, Process, Physical evidence.

subject: business
title: Break-Even Analysis
stem_template: Which of the following statements about break-even analysis are TRUE? Select ALL that apply.

options_template.text: At break-even, total revenue equals total costs
Above break-even output, the firm makes a loss
Fixed costs do not change with output level
Break-even output = Fixed Costs ÷ Contribution per unit
A lower selling price always reduces the break-even quantity

mark_scheme_template: TRUE: TR=TC at break-even; FC constant; BEQ=FC÷(P-VC). FALSE: above BEQ = profit (not loss); lower price reduces contribution per unit → INCREASES BEQ.
hints: Break-even: no profit, no loss.
Contribution per unit = Selling price - Variable cost per unit.

subject: business
title: Organisational Structures
stem_template: Which of the following are TRUE about a TALL organisational structure? Select ALL that apply.

options_template.text: There are many levels of hierarchy
Each manager has a wide span of control
Communication from top to bottom may be slower
It tends to be more bureaucratic
It is better suited to creative, innovative firms

mark_scheme_template: Tall = many levels; NARROW span of control; slower vertical communication; more bureaucratic. FALSE: narrow (not wide) span; flat structures suit creative/innovative environments.
hints: Tall = more levels, narrow span.
Flat = fewer levels, wide span.

subject: business
title: Investment Appraisal — Payback Period Steps
stem_template: Arrange the following steps for calculating the PAYBACK PERIOD of an investment in the CORRECT ORDER.
items: Calculate cumulative cash flows year by year
Identify the year when cumulative cash flow turns positive
Calculate the exact payback month within the final year
mark_scheme_template: Correct order: (1) identify initial cost; (2) calculate cumulative CF; (3) find when CF turns positive; (4) calculate exact month within that year.
hints: Start with initial cost (year 0).
Payback month = remaining cost ÷ that year's CF × 12.
